cases.title: Endometrial biopsy: atypical hyperplasia / EIN
cases.clinicalHistory: 54-year-old woman with abnormal uterine bleeding and thickened endometrium on ultrasound.

image.caption: Crowded endometrial glands with cytologic demarcation from background endometrium.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: histology of early biopsies with ihc confirmed hts mutations but lacking diagnostic features of ein ah all panels are at the same magnification bar 100 m images were taken with 20 objective cd glands per ihc step sections are shown with symbol a representative endometrial cancer pt3 figo grade 1 for general comparison showing crowded glands with no intervening stroma diagnostic of adenocarcinoma b pt3 most fragments are superficial and atrophic rare glands in retrospect have distinct histology but such glands are too few and changes too minimal for diagnosis of ah ein c pt11 glands have some crowding and architectural abnormalities that fall short of ah ein criteria d pt13 glands have larger cells but minimal architectural irregularities e pt14 specimen very scant and highly fragmented limiting diagnosis f pt21 glands show mild architectural abnormalities and are not histologically very distinct from neighboring glands

diagnosticSteps: Identify architecturally crowded glands with gland area exceeding stroma in a discrete focus.
Confirm cytologic atypia relative to background endometrium and exclude benign mimics such as breakdown or polyp effect.
Search for confluent, complex, or solid growth that would support endometrioid carcinoma rather than EIN alone.

ancillaryOptions.name: PTEN and PAX2
ancillaryOptions.result: Loss may support EIN in a difficult or fragmented biopsy but is not required when morphology is diagnostic.

ancillaryOptions.name: Mismatch repair IHC
ancillaryOptions.result: Not routinely required for isolated EIN on biopsy unless carcinoma is diagnosed or clinical criteria indicate testing.

ancillaryOptions.name: p53
ancillaryOptions.result: Wild-type pattern would not support serous carcinoma; reserve for marked atypia or serous differential.

cases.reportingTarget: Endometrium, biopsy: Atypical hyperplasia / endometrioid intraepithelial neoplasia (EIN). No carcinoma identified in this sample.
pitfalls: Do not overcall gland crowding from collapse, metaplasia, or polyp-associated change as EIN.
Do not exclude carcinoma if the biopsy is scant and clinically suspicious; communicate sampling limitation.

cases.title: Endometrial biopsy: serous carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 69-year-old postmenopausal woman with bleeding and a heterogeneous endometrial cavity on ultrasound.

image.caption: High-grade endometrial carcinoma with papillary architecture and marked cytologic atypia.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: histological findings of endometrium a high grade serous carcinoma has an infiltrated growth pattern in myometrial muscle the neoplastic cells are arranged in nesting glandular and ethmoid with abundant cytoplasm partial eosinophilic large nucleus obvious heterogeneity and prominent nucleolus h e 100 b neuroendocrine carcinoma is arranged in trabeculae and islands the tumor cells are large with rich cytoplasm and the nucleus is pleomorphic arranged in a mosaic and the nucleolus is obvious h e 100 c the adenocarcinoma manifested positive for pax 8 d synaptophysin immunolabeling of neuroendocrine carcinoma e chromogranin a immunolabeling of neuroendocrine carcinoma f negative immunohistochemical stains for pax 8 in neuroendocrine carcinoma

diagnosticSteps: Recognize high-grade cytology with complex papillary, glandular, or solid architecture out of proportion to endometrioid carcinoma grade 1-2.
Evaluate for serous endometrial intraepithelial carcinoma and background atrophic endometrium when present.
Use p53 and p16 to support serous differentiation and avoid misclassifying a high-grade endometrioid carcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: p53 and p16
ancillaryOptions.result: Aberrant p53 with diffuse p16 supports serous carcinoma in the appropriate morphology.

ancillaryOptions.name: ER and PR
ancillaryOptions.result: Often reduced or patchy in serous carcinoma; useful as supportive context, not a standalone classifier.

ancillaryOptions.name: Mismatch repair IHC
ancillaryOptions.result: May be performed for endometrial carcinoma screening but does not replace histotype assignment.

pitfalls: Do not diagnose serous carcinoma from p53 staining alone without compatible morphology.
Do not miss mixed carcinoma if a low-grade endometrioid component is also present.

cases.title: Hysterectomy: endometrioid carcinoma with myoinvasion
cases.clinicalHistory: 61-year-old woman with biopsy-proven FIGO grade 1 endometrioid adenocarcinoma.

sourceArticle.citation: Int J Gynecol Pathol. 2019 Jan 14; 38(Iss 1 Suppl 1):S9-S24
sourceArticle.figureCaption: inking the anterior and posterior uterine serosal surfaces with extension of the ink to the ectocervix or vaginal cuff margin a helps to confirm the presence of tumor in the uterine serosa arrow b and in cases with cervical stromal involvement c the measurement of the relationship of the stromal invasion to the full thickness of the cervical wall line and the proper identification of the ectocervical or vaginal cuff margin d

diagnosticSteps: Confirm endometrioid histotype and assign FIGO grade based on solid growth and nuclear features.
Measure tumor size and depth of myometrial invasion relative to total myometrial thickness.
Assess cervical stromal involvement, lymphovascular invasion, lower uterine segment, adnexa, serosa, margins, and submitted lymph nodes.

ancillaryOptions.name: Mismatch repair IHC
ancillaryOptions.result: Screening panel helps identify mismatch repair deficiency and potential Lynch syndrome workup.

ancillaryOptions.name: p53
ancillaryOptions.result: Wild-type pattern supports low-grade endometrioid carcinoma when morphology is concordant.

ancillaryOptions.name: ER and PR
ancillaryOptions.result: Usually positive in low-grade endometrioid carcinoma; not required for routine staging.

cases.reportingTarget: Uterus, cervix, bilateral tubes and ovaries, hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy: Endometrioid carcinoma, FIGO grade 1, 4.2 cm, invading 6 mm of 18 mm myometrium, no cervical stromal involvement, no lymphovascular invasion identified, margins negative, pT1a.
pitfalls: Do not report percent myoinvasion without documenting both invasion depth and myometrial thickness.
Do not omit LVSI because it affects risk stratification and adjuvant management.

cases.title: Cervical cone: HSIL with endocervical adenocarcinoma in situ
cases.specimenType: Cold knife cone biopsy with endocervical curettage
cases.clinicalHistory: 37-year-old woman with HPV-16 positivity, HSIL cytology, and atypical glandular cells.

image.caption: High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion with adjacent endocervical glandular atypia.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: management of women with atypical glandular cells not otherwise specified cin2 cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 2 leep loop electrosurgical excision procedure ckc cold knife conization ais adenocarcinoma in situ ecc endocervical curettage

diagnosticSteps: Map HSIL extent across ectocervical and endocervical quadrants and identify gland duct involvement if present.
Evaluate endocervical glands for AIS with stratified atypical mucinous epithelium and mitotic/apoptotic activity.
Assess ectocervical, endocervical, and deep margin status separately and exclude stromal invasion.

ancillaryOptions.name: p16
ancillaryOptions.result: Block-type staining supports HPV-associated HSIL/AIS in morphologically suspicious foci.

ancillaryOptions.name: Ki-67
ancillaryOptions.result: Expanded proliferation supports HSIL when the differential includes immature metaplasia.

ancillaryOptions.name: CEA and ER
ancillaryOptions.result: Can help separate AIS from endometrial-type glandular mimics in selected cases.

cases.reportingTarget: Cervix, cold knife cone: High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (CIN 3) and endocervical adenocarcinoma in situ. No invasive carcinoma identified. Endocervical margin involved by AIS; ectocervical and deep margins negative. Endocervical curettage shows AIS.
pitfalls: Do not combine all margins into a single positive/negative statement.
Do not miss skip lesions of AIS; submit and examine the cone systematically.

cases.title: Oophorectomy: serous borderline tumor
cases.clinicalHistory: 45-year-old woman with a 7.5 cm multiloculated cystic ovarian mass and no radiographic extraovarian disease.

image.caption: Serous borderline tumor with hierarchical papillary architecture and epithelial stratification.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: questions that need to be discussed step by step by the mdt for embryo cryopreservation before fss in bot patients and our answers for the patient in this case mdt multidisciplinary team fss fertility sparing surgery bot borderline ovarian tumor fp fertility preservation re fss re fertility sparing surgery le letrozole os ovarian stimulation opu oocyte pick up fet frozen embryo transfer

diagnosticSteps: Identify hierarchical papillary architecture with epithelial proliferation and mild to moderate atypia.
Extensively sample solid, papillary, or excrescences to exclude invasive low-grade serous carcinoma.
Report ovarian surface involvement, capsule status, implants if submitted, and microinvasion or micropapillary pattern if present.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Typical serous borderline tumor is diagnosed by morphology and sampling.

ancillaryOptions.name: WT1 and PAX8
ancillaryOptions.result: Supports Mullerian serous lineage if the differential includes metastatic carcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: p53
ancillaryOptions.result: Wild-type pattern is expected; aberrant staining should prompt reconsideration of high-grade serous carcinoma.

cases.reportingTarget: Ovary and fallopian tube, unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy: Serous borderline tumor, 7.5 cm, confined to ovary, ovarian surface not involved, no microinvasion identified, fallopian tube uninvolved, pT1a.
pitfalls: Do not under-sample papillary excrescences where invasive carcinoma may be focal.
Do not call detached epithelial clusters in cyst fluid invasive implants.

cases.title: Products of conception: first-trimester evacuation
cases.specimenType: Suction dilation and curettage products of conception
cases.clinicalHistory: 29-year-old woman with missed abortion at 9 weeks gestational age.

image.caption: Immature chorionic villi and decidua in products of conception.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: retained products of conception a and b prehysteroscopic morcellation and c and d postmorcellation appearance with the intrauterine bigatti shaver system

diagnosticSteps: Confirm that the specimen grossly contains villous or membranous tissue consistent with products of conception.
Submit representative tissue when local policy requires microscopic confirmation or when villi are not grossly identified.
Screen for hydropic villi, abnormal trophoblastic proliferation, fetal parts, or clinical concern for ectopic pregnancy.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: No ancillary study is needed for routine first-trimester products with typical villi.

ancillaryOptions.name: p57
ancillaryOptions.result: Reserve for suspected complete hydatidiform mole or abnormal villous morphology.

ancillaryOptions.name: Molar genotyping
ancillaryOptions.result: Useful when morphology and p57 do not resolve molar classification.

grossOnlyChecklist: representative sampling or gross-only policy statement
cases.reportingTarget: Uterine contents, suction curettage: Products of conception, immature chorionic villi and decidua identified.
pitfalls: Do not issue a routine products diagnosis if no villi are identified and ectopic pregnancy is a clinical concern.
Do not miss molar pregnancy in hydropic or unusually proliferative villi.
